properties.summary: George Street is Australia's oldest street, the long spine that runs through the heart of Sydney from the Rocks down to Brickfield Hill. For more than two centuries it has been the city's main thoroughfare, carrying convicts, carriages, trams and shoppers in turn.

timeline.event: A rough track is worn along the western side of the Tank Stream, linking the Rocks to the brickfields. Early colonists know it as Spring Row, after the water carriers who walked it, and it becomes the first street of the new colony.
Governor Macquarie straightens and widens the road and gives it a proper name, George Street, after King George III. It is among the first of his ordered, named streets.

Sydney's first horse trams open, though they run along Pitt Street rather than George Street. George Street has to wait until 1899 for its own line, when electric trams arrive and make it the city's busiest transport artery.
The great emporiums and arcades rise along the street, among them the Queen Victoria Building, the Strand Arcade and Anthony Hordern's. George Street becomes the retail heart of the colony.

Light rail returns to a newly pedestrianised George Street, with the line opening in December 2019, closing a long loop back to its days as a tram street. The old electric trams had run here from 1899 until 1959.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; City of Sydney
properties.name: George Street
properties.image_caption: George Street today, light rail returned to Sydney's oldest street

properties.original_use: Road to the South Head signal station
properties.summary: Old South Head Road is the route Governor Macquarie had cut from the town out to South Head in 1811. It was built to reach the signal station that flagged arriving ships, and soon served the lighthouse that guarded the harbour mouth.

timeline.event: Macquarie has a road cut from the town to South Head, so that news of ships flagged at the signal station can reach Sydney quickly.
The road serves Francis Greenway's new Macquarie Lighthouse at Vaucluse, the first lighthouse in Australia, lit for the first time in November.
Grand marine villas and estates rise along the route at Vaucluse, Rose Bay and Watsons Bay, as the wealthy claim the harbour-mouth heights.
The Watsons Bay tramway is extended out to Gap Park near South Head, carrying day-trippers to the eastern headlands and beaches.

properties.name: Old South Head Road

properties.summary: Bridge Street takes its name from the first bridge thrown across the Tank Stream, built in the colony's earliest months. It ran up to First Government House and became the administrative spine of early Sydney.

timeline.event: A timber bridge is built across the Tank Stream in the colony's first year, the first bridge in New South Wales, near where Pitt Street now crosses.
First Government House, finished around 1788 to 1789, stands at the head of the street as the home and seat of the early governors, with the bridge linking it to the working town.
Governor Macquarie formally names the street Bridge Street after that early Tank Stream crossing.
Grand offices for departments such as Lands, Treasury and the Chief Secretary, built across the 1840s to 1890s, give Bridge Street its monumental character.

properties.name: Bridge Street

properties.summary: William Street climbs from Hyde Park up to Kings Cross, taking its name from King William IV. It began as the road out to the windmills and villas of Darlinghurst and later became Sydney's neon-lit gateway to the Cross.

timeline.event: A road is cut up the ridge from the town towards the windmills, farms and grand villas of Darlinghurst and Woolloomooloo. It is soon named after King William IV.
The City Council resolves to widen William Street from about 40 feet to 100 feet, hoping to turn it into a dignified boulevard, a kind of Sydney answer to the Champs-Elysees.

By now the William Street and Kings Cross junction is one of the city's busiest corners, with trams running roughly a minute apart and the street settling into its role as the bright, slightly raffish way into the Cross.

properties.name: William Street

properties.summary: Broadway is the broad gateway where George Street runs into Parramatta Road, on the edge of Glebe and Chippendale. For much of the last century it was known for the Grace Bros emporium on one side and the great Kent Brewery on the other.

timeline.event: This low, often flooded stretch of Parramatta Street, near the old Blackwattle Swamp, carries traffic out of the town towards Glebe and Chippendale.
Grace Bros opens its grand new store with its landmark clock tower, turning the street into one of Sydney's busiest shopping destinations.
Tooth and Co's Kent Brewery, on the site since 1835, looms over the Chippendale side as one of the street's defining landmarks through the 20th century.

properties.name: Broadway

properties.summary: Macquarie Street is Sydney's grand government street, running along the ridge east of the old Tank Stream. Governor Macquarie laid it out as the dignified face of his colony, and it gathered the public buildings, the parliament and, later, the consulting rooms of the city's leading doctors. It remains the address where the business of the state and the work of the specialists sit side by side.

timeline.event: Macquarie lays out a broad street along the ridge east of the Tank Stream, meant as the dignified face of his improving colony.
The Rum Hospital is completed on the street, and Greenway's own Georgian buildings soon follow nearby, the Hyde Park Barracks from 1817 and St James' Church from 1819.
Having housed the Legislative Council since 1829, a wing of the old Rum Hospital now opens as the seat of the new bicameral NSW Parliament.
The upper end fills with the rooms of Sydney's leading doctors and specialists, earning it a reputation as the Harley Street of the south.

properties.name: Macquarie Street
properties.image_caption: Macquarie Street and its sandstone government buildings

properties.summary: Victoria Road is the main north-western route out of the inner west, running through Drummoyne and Gladesville on its way towards Ryde and Parramatta. For much of its length it follows old colonial lines towards the orchards and farms of the Ryde district.

timeline.event: There is still no bridge here. Punts and ferries carry people and carts across the Parramatta River while small villages take shape along the road to Ryde.

The first Gladesville Bridge opens on 1 February, a steel bridge with a swing span that lets tall ships through. It is the only crossing of the Parramatta River below Parramatta and opens the north-west to closer settlement.
A vast new concrete-arch Gladesville Bridge carries the road high over the river. On completion it has the longest single concrete arch span in the world, a record it holds for about fifteen years.

properties.name: Victoria Road

properties.original_use: Ridge road to the eastern suburbs
properties.summary: Oxford Street follows the old sandy ridge that early colonists turned into the road out to South Head. Convicts cleared the track in 1803, and by the 1880s it had become one of Sydney's busiest shopping strips outside the city. Since the 1970s it has been the heart of gay Sydney and the home of Mardi Gras.

timeline.event: The track along the sandy ridge towards South Head, cleared by convicts in 1803, is properly formed for wheeled traffic. The Hyde Park to Paddington section is renamed Oxford Street in 1875, borrowing the name of the famous London shopping street.
A horse tramline opens along the road, and by the end of the decade Oxford Street is one of Sydney's most prominent shopping high streets.
On the night of 24 June the first Sydney Gay Mardi Gras heads down Oxford Street. Police block the crowd and arrest 53 people, and the street becomes the symbolic home of the movement.
The Mardi Gras parade, now a huge public celebration, fills Oxford Street each year, and the strip is the recognised heart of LGBTQ Sydney.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Pride History Group
properties.name: Oxford Street

properties.summary: King Street is Newtown's long Victorian high street, running along the old southern road out of Sydney. It is widely regarded as the most complete late-Victorian and Federation shopping strip in Australia, saved largely because the suburb was for years too unfashionable to bother redeveloping.

timeline.event: With the railway through Newtown open since 1855, shops and terraces are crowding in along King Street, the old road south out of the city.
An almost unbroken run of ornate Victorian shopfronts now lines the street, serving a dense working-class suburb.
King Street has become a bohemian strip of cafes, bookshops, music venues and street art, and is now the spine of the inner west.

properties.name: King Street, Newtown

properties.summary: Pitt Street is one of Sydney's oldest streets and a long-running commercial heart of the city, running south from near the old Tank Stream down to the modern Pitt Street Mall.

timeline.event: An early track runs roughly parallel to George Street, along the eastern side of the Tank Stream.
By Macquarie's time the street is well established. It was named in the colony's first years, as Pitt Row, in honour of British Prime Minister William Pitt the Younger.
Electric trams, grand banks and big stores have made Pitt Street the busy commercial and financial spine of the city.
The block between King and Market Streets closes to traffic as the Pitt Street Mall, one of the busiest shopping strips in the country.

properties.name: Pitt Street

properties.summary: Parramatta Road is the oldest highway in Australia, the roughly 23 kilometre link between the colony's first two towns. Convicts first cut it as a narrow track through the bush around 1789, and Macquarie rebuilt it as the country's first toll road in 1811. In the motor age it became Sydney's great strip of car showrooms.

timeline.event: Convict gangs begin cutting a narrow track through the bush between Sydney Cove and the new farming settlement at Rose Hill, later Parramatta. It is the colony's first inland road.
Macquarie has the road properly formed and turns it into a turnpike, the first toll road in Australia, opened on 10 April with tollgates and milestones.
Coaching inns line the route, and a regular coach service runs between the two towns. The cart journey that once took a full day becomes far quicker.
Horse-bus and later tram services run the road as ribbon suburbs such as Annandale, Leichhardt and Petersham fill the corridor between the towns.
The motor age turns Parramatta Road into Sydney's great strip of car showrooms and signage. It is later bypassed by the M4 motorway.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Roads & Maritime Services
properties.name: Parramatta Road
properties.image_caption: Parramatta Road, the colony's first highway

properties.summary: Anzac Parade is Sydney's grand ceremonial avenue running south-east from Moore Park. It began as Randwick Road, the way out to the racecourse and the camps on the sandhills, and was renamed in 1917 to honour the Australian and New Zealand Army Corps.

timeline.event: A road is formed south from Moore Park as the way to the revived Randwick Racecourse and the rifle ranges and camps on the sandhills beyond.
Thousands of soldiers march down the avenue from the army camps at Kensington and the showground, heading for the wharves to embark for the Great War.
The thoroughfare is renamed Anzac Parade and a sandstone obelisk goes up at its head, one of the country's earliest Anzac memorials and older than the Martin Place Cenotaph.
Light rail returns to the parade, with the line to Randwick opening late in the year and the Kingsford branch following in 2020.

properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Randwick City Council
properties.name: Anzac Parade
properties.image_caption: Anzac Parade running south through Kingsford and Maroubra

properties.summary: Military Road began as the track used to reach the gun batteries guarding the harbour at Middle and Georges Heads, and it is now the shopping spine of Mosman and the lower north shore.

timeline.event: A rough track is cut across the peninsula towards the new harbour batteries at Middle and Georges Heads, the route along which gun barrels are said to have been hauled. This track is the ancestor of Military Road.
An electric tramway opens along Military Road in September, the first permanent electric tram line in New South Wales, and it begins drawing settlers out to Mosman, Cremorne and Neutral Bay.
A continuous strip of shops has grown along the road, turning it into the high street of the lower north shore.

properties.name: Military Road
